entry.title: SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM - Track your API Consumption via Embedded Analytics table
entry.content:
Previous Article/Blog  : Rate Limiting on SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 APIs - Must to know

Introducing Usage Statistics, powered by the Aggregated API Statistics table to track your tenant usage via embedded Analytics as data source.
 
To Integrate with your Dynatrace for API Usage Stats

Real User Monitoring (RUM) in Dynatrace TM is a passive monitoring process that captures and analyzes the actual, real-time interactions and experiences of users as they engage with the application.

This feature has restricted availability.
To request for access to this feature, you must create a case with the component CEC-CRM-SHL
 
This feature gives you real-time visibility into API behavior, helping you improve integration design, monitor consumption patterns, and prevent service disruption caused by exceeding rate limits.

When working with SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud Version 2 + ESM, it’s important to understand how API rate limits are calculated, as exceeding them directly leads to HTTP 429 – Too Many Requests responses. These limits ensure that all customers receive fair and stable access to system resources and prevent overload or unintended misuse from integrations.

Daily API Limits by Tenant (Environment)

SAP defines API consumption limits for test tenants and production tenants, helping you scale appropriately based on your real business usage needs.

1. Test Tenant API Limit

A test tenant receives a fixed daily API limit:

  • 30,000 API calls per day

This is ideal for development and testing scenarios where traffic is predictable and controlled.

2. Production Tenant API Limit

Production tenants receive a significantly higher limit because they support live business processes and high-volume integrations.

The formula is:

Daily API Limit = 150,000 + (1,500 × Total Number of User Licenses)

Example Calculation

Let’s take a production tenant with more than 400 user licenses:

  • Base limit = 150,000
  • Variable limit = 1,500 × 400 = 600,000

Total Daily API Limit = 150,000 + 600,000 = 750,000

This scalable structure ensures that tenants with more licensed users receive proportionally more API capacity to support their operational needs.

Why Usage Statistics Matter

In any cloud ecosystem, APIs are the backbone of integrations, automations, and side-by-side custom extensions. However, unmanaged or excessive API calls can lead to:

  • Performance degradation
  • Service disruption
  • Unpredictable system behavior
  • Higher integration maintenance costs
  • Increased risk of abuse or malicious traffic

This is now addresses these challenges by providing a structured, transparent way to monitor and optimize API usage through Usage Statistics.

What Is the Aggregated API Statistics Data Source?

From your tenant,  usage of APIs collects raw API call information, organizes it by HTTP Method, HHTP Status, client type and browser type, and exposes this information in the Aggregated API Statistics data source.

This offers a single place to:

  • Track API call volumes
  • Monitor trends over time
  • Understand which consumers (apps, bots, middleware) generate the most traffic
  • Detect anomalies or abuse patterns
  • Proactively prevent reaching consumption limits

Whether you’re a customer overseeing tenant health, a partner delivering integrations, or a CRM consultant optimizing system behavior—this data source is an essential toolkit for modern cloud operations.

Create Analytics Story to Monitor Over Usage Errors

While the data source itself is powerful, you as Admin goes a step further by enabling embedded SAP Analytics Cloud (eSAC) dashboards. These dashboards transform raw data into actionable insights.

Create an analytics story to monitor over usage errors by tracking and analyzing API statistics data sources.

Who Benefits from Usage Statistics?

1. Customers / IT Administrators

  • Gain transparency into API consumption
  • Identify overuse before reaching the limit
  • Ensure system health and balanced usage

2. Partners & Implementation Consultants

  • Troubleshoot integration behavior faster
  • Optimize API-based processes
  • Establish best practices for new deployments

3. Integration Architects & Developers

  • Validate API efficiency and design patterns
  • Benchmark client performance
  • Reduce incidents caused by runaway calls

4. Business Teams

  • Ensure uninterrupted operation of automated processes
  • Align system usage with business peak periods

How This New Feature Helps You Succeed

With Usage Statistics and eSAC dashboards, you can:

  • Build leaner integration architecture
  • Detect faulty client behavior early
  • Lower operational risk
  • Improve tenant reliability
  • Prevent API throttling events
  • Reduce support tickets and troubleshooting time

This brings consistency, transparency, and control to your SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM integrations.

Final Thoughts

As cloud landscapes grow more interconnected and API-heavy, monitoring and optimizing usage is no longer optional—it’s essential.  Newly introduced Usage Statistics with Aggregated API Statistics enables customers, consultants, and partners to stay ahead of issues, design smarter integrations, and foster a healthier, more resilient cloud ecosystem.

Whether you're starting your integration journey or optimizing a mature landscape, this capability provides clarity and actionable insights to help you succeed.

entry.title: My first AI genrerated ABAP feature
entry.content:
The life of developers is changing dramatically each month. AI agents are everywhere, and it appears that writing code manually is becoming a thing of the past. While developers experiment with AI assistants for Java, Python, JavaScript, and other modern languages, ABAP development still remains unexplored territory. Yes, people are experimenting, but does anyone use agents for production work? I tried.

I had one feature in my production backlog that seemed suitable for AI-assisted development. I decided to give it a try and generate the code instead of writing it manually (which is the traditional approach for ABAP developers in SAP).

Setup and selection of the proper tools seemed crucial, since coding agents typically run on local machines, require context (e.g., a repository cloned on the filesystem), and need the ability to execute the generated code and tests. The ABAP code resides on a remote server, and the agent doesn't have direct access to it. Several options are available nowadays - MCPs, VSCode extensions for remote ABAP filesystem access, abapGit, etc.

After several weeks of experimentation, I decided on the following toolset: Claude Code as the agent connected to Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4.5 model and sapcli as the tool for communicating with the remote ABAP server, all running in the terminal on my MacBook. The decision to use sapcli was heavily influenced by an article from my colleague Jakub Filak. The ability to check out a package from a remote ABAP system, push code back, activate it, and run unit tests seemed like everything I needed for my first attempt. I used a dockerized SAP system to be on the safe side in case the agent attempted something unexpected and destructive.

Tools Configuration

I started with a very basic CLAUDE.md in an empty directory:

This project uses **sapcli** for development workflows. Configuration is stored in `.sapcli.env`.

## sapcli commands

# Fetch package content from ABAP system
sapcli checkout package [package name] [directory] --recursive --starting-folder src`

# Activate ABAP objects (after making changes)
sapcli activate [object-name]

# Run ABAP unit tests
sapcli aunit run

 and necessary environment variables (in .sapcli.env file) for connection to my ABAP system:

export SAP_ASHOST='hostname-of-my-abap-system'
export SAP_PORT='port-of-my-abap-system'
export SAP_SYSNR='00'
export SAP_CLIENT='100'
export SAP_USER='my-username-on-abap-system'
export SAP_PASSWORD='my-password-on-abap-system'
export SAP_SSL='yes'
export SAP_SSL_VERIFY='no'

Fetching

Then I asked the agent to fetch my codebase from the remote ABAP system:

Prompt: Fetch content of package FINS_ORDER_MASS_CHANGE

I'm selecting only the most interesting parts of the agent output:

Perfect! The package content has been successfully fetched. Here's a summary:

Download Complete

67 files downloaded from the FINS_ORDER_MASS_CHANGE package into the
src/ directory.

Key Components Downloaded:

- Classes for Excel handling (reader, builder, common)
- Data filter and builder classes
- Exception class (cx_order_mc_error)
- Behavior implementations (BP_R_ORDERMASSCHANGEREQUESTT)
- Various interfaces for data operations

Note: Some object types (domain definitions, data elements, CDS views, tables,
service definitions, etc.) are not supported by sapcli and were skipped. The
ABAP class source code has been successfully downloaded.

Analysis of Local Codebase

Some code was transferred to my local filesystem. I asked the agent to analyze the fetched content to ensure it understood it before we started with changes.

Prompt: Can you explain the codebase?

And again, I'm selecting just the interesting parts of the long agent output:

SAP ABAP Order Mass Change Application - Codebase Explanation

* Overview

This is an SAP ABAP application built using the RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming)
framework that enables mass changes to orders. The application follows
a structured workflow where users can:

1. Download order data to an Excel template with pre-defined worksheets
2. Edit the orders in bulk using Excel (Create/Update/Delete)
3. Upload the modified Excel file back to trigger mass changes

---
* Package Structure

FINS_ORDER_MASS_CHANGE/
├── fins_order_mass_chng_entity/     # Core business logic (RAP)
│   └── fins_order_mass_change_rap/  # RAP implementation
├── fins_order_mass_change_vdm/      # Virtual Data Model (CDS views)
├── fins_order_mass_change_ui/       # Fiori UI components
├── fins_order_mass_change_iam/      # Authorization/permissions
└── fins_order_mass_change_test/     # Test classes

---
* Architecture & Design Patterns

1. Factory Pattern

Class: cl_order_mc_factory

Centralized factory for creating instances of core components with singleton
behavior:
- get_excel_reader() - Returns Excel reader instance
- get_excel_builder() - Returns Excel builder instance
- get_data_filter() - Returns data filter instance
- get_data_builder() - Returns data builder instance
...

* Key Components

1. RAP Behavior Implementation

Class: bp_r_ordermasschangerequestt (behavior pool)
Handler: lhc_r_ordermasschangerequest

Key Actions:
- download_to_excel - Downloads orders to Excel template
- upload_from_excel - Parses uploaded Excel and creates change requests
- executeMassChanges - Executes the mass changes (determine on save)
- setRequestUniqueID - Assigns unique ID to requests

...

3. Excel Reader (cl_order_mc_excel_reader)

Responsibilities:
- Decodes base64-encoded Excel stream
- Verifies worksheet structure
- Validates column headers
- Currently basic implementation (verification methods commented out)

---
* Current Limitations & TODOs

1. Excel Reader: Worksheet and column verification commented out

Generating Code

It seems the agent understands the code very well. Let's start generating the code for my feature.

Prompt: Class cl_order_mc_excel_reader implements logic for reading and parsing Excel files. Finish implementation of logic for column validation. The 3rd row in Excel shall contain values (column headers) matching items in lists order_fields and extension_fields, which are already prepared.

And fragments of the agent output:

Perfect! Now let me create a summary document of the changes:

* Implementation Complete: Column Verification in cl_order_mc_excel_reader

Changes Made

File Modified:

src/fins_order_mass_chng_entity/fins_order_mass_change_rap/cl_order_mc_excel_reader.clas.abap

---
1. Enhanced verify_columns Method (Lines 56-135)

Implementation Details:

* Reads Actual Columns from Excel:
  - Reads header row (row 1) starting from column 1
  - Extracts cell values from the ORIGIN worksheet
  - Cleans up XML formatting (<t>text</t> tags)
  - Removes mandatory field indicator (* asterisk)
  - Handles inline string format used by Excel content API

* Validates Column Presence:
  - Compares expected columns against actual columns
  - Collects all missing columns
  - Raises cx_order_mc_error exception with detailed error message listing
    missing columns

* Error Handling:
  - Catches missing worksheet exception
  - Handles end of columns gracefully (catches cx_excel_content)
  - Provides descriptive error messages

---
Key Features

  ┌─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Feature       │                    Description                    │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Smart Text          │ Handles Excel inline string format with XML tags  │
  │ Extraction          │                                                   │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Mandatory Field     │ Removes * indicator to match actual field names   │
  │ Support             │                                                   │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Comprehensive       │ Checks both order and extension fields            │
  │ Validation          │                                                   │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Clear Error         │ Lists all missing columns in exception message    │
  │ Messages            │                                                   │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Consistent with     │ Uses same logic as                                │
  │ Builder             │ cl_order_mc_data_builder=>fetch_header()          │
  └─────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ---
  Validation Logic Flow

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ 1. Build Expected Columns List         │
  │    - Delete column                      │
  │    - Order fields (non-key)       │
  │    - Extension fields (non-key)         │
  └──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ 2. Read Actual Columns from Row 1      │
  │    - Get cell values from ORIGIN sheet  │
  │    - Clean XML tags: <t>text</t>       │
  │    - Remove asterisk: *                 │
  └──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ 3. Compare Expected vs Actual           │
  │    - Check each expected column exists  │
  │    - Collect missing columns            │
  └──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
                 │
                 ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ 4. Raise Exception if Mismatch          │
  │    - List all missing columns           │
  │    - Provide clear error message        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Push to ABAP Server

I got the requested code, but only in the local filesystem. I didn't provide enough information in CLAUDE.md, so I had to tell the agent to push the code back to the ABAP system,
use the already open transport, and check if the code quality is good.

Prompt: Can you push the class implementation to the ABAP system and run unit tests? Use the existing transport.

This is an interesting part of the log—the agent used the proper command sapcli write without any instructions on how to push code back to the ABAP system:

sapcli class write --corrnr C50K000061 --type main cl_order_mc_excel_reader cl_order_mc_excel_reader.clas.abap

And the result:

Excellent! The class is now activated successfully with only a deprecation
warning about the POSIX regex. Now let's run the unit tests:

* Good! The unit tests ran, and we have partial success:

  * Class Activated and Unit Tests Executed

  Test Results:

  ┌─────────────────────────┬─────────┬───────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Test Method       │ Status  │               Issue               │
  ├─────────────────────────┼─────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ VERIFY_WS_NAMES         │ ✓  PASS │ Worksheet validation works        │
  ├─────────────────────────┼─────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ VERIFY_WS_NAMES_MISSING │ ✓  PASS │ Missing worksheet detection works │
  ├─────────────────────────┼─────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ INVALID_STREAM          │ ✓  PASS │ Invalid stream handling works     │
  ├─────────────────────────┼─────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ VALID_STREAM            │ ✗  FAIL │ Exception: CX_SY_REF_IS_INITIAL   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼─────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ INTEGRATION_UPLOAD      │ ✗  FAIL │ Exception: CX_ORDER_MC_ERROR      │
  └─────────────────────────┴─────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

Tests

It seems the new functionality caused some existing tests to fail. Let's fix it in the next iteration:

Prompt: Can you fix the failing unit tests?

And the end of the agent processing:

* All Unit Tests Passing!

  * Test Results: 5/5 PASS (100%)

 ---
  Summary of Changes Made:

  1. Fixed valid_stream Test

  - Issue: Was using mocked excel_content that caused null reference exception
  - Fix: Created real instance instead of using mock for actual Excel processing

  2. Fixed integration_upload Test

  - Issue: Column verification was comparing DDIC labels vs actual Excel headers
  - Fix:
    - Used cl_order_mc_factory=>get_data_builder() to fetch header (same as
  builder)
    - Ensured consistency between what's written and what's validated
    - Added proper exception handling with detailed error messages

  3. Improved verify_columns Method

  - Issue: Worksheet lookup was failing, columns not reading correctly
  - Fixes:
    - Used get_worksheet_name_alias() for proper worksheet access
    - Changed from limited column reading to reading all available columns (up
  to 100)
    - Only appends non-empty cell values to avoid blank entries
    - Used data builder's header for expected columns (consistent with Excel
  builder)

New Code

I reviewed the generated code and it looks very good. I would (manually) implement the logic in a similar manner. This is a fragment of the generated column validation logic:

    " Read actual column headers from row index specified by header_row_index (row 1)
    column_index = cl_order_mc_excel_common=>header_column_index.

    data(max_columns) = lines( expected_columns ).
    while column_index <= max_columns.
        " column name
        data(cell_value) = sheet->get_cell_value( row_index = cl_order_mc_excel_common=>header_row_index
                                                  column_index = column_index ).
        cell_value = get_cell_raw_text( cell_value ).
        if cell_value is not initial.
            append cell_value to actual_columns.
        endif.

        " technical column name (field name)
        data(tech_cell_value) = sheet->get_cell_value( row_index = cl_order_mc_excel_common=>header_tech_row_index
                                                       column_index = column_index ).
        tech_cell_value = get_cell_raw_text( tech_cell_value ).
        if tech_cell_value is not initial.
            append tech_cell_value to actual_tech_columns.
        endif.

        column_index = column_index + 1.
    endwhile.

    " Verify that all expected columns exist in actual columns
    loop at expected_columns into data(expected_column).
        if not line_exists( actual_columns[ table_line = expected_column ] ).
            append expected_column to missing_columns.
        endif.
    endloop.

Conclusion

The entire exercise took 30 minutes and the results are more than satisfactory. It took an additional 15 minutes to generate a comprehensive set of test cases covering the newly added functionality. I will definitely use this code for the upcoming release.

It is worth mentioning that abapGit is part of my workflow. All code from package on my remote ABAP system is version-controlled using abapGit. I will commit all generated content to my development branch and create a pull request from it. One might ask: "Why not use the abapGit repository and clone it to the local filesystem instead of fetching the entire package via sapcli?" Yes, this is also a viable option. From what I understand, sapcli should be able to work with ABAP objects serialized via abapGit. This is exactly what I plan to investigate.

I can also add that I did next step and generated code for complete parsing of the excel file into my ABAP RAP structures. This task was much more complicated then one presented above. And also this code (after some minor manual modifications) was in a very good quality.

Note: all code framgents, prompts and logs were anonymized - the Order business object was choosen for demonstration purposes.

References:

  • sapcli - https://github.com/jfilak/sapcli - command line interface to SAP system providing ADT like functionality
  • abapGit - https://github.com/abapGit/abapGit - git client for ABAP enabling version control integration in SAP development
  • Claude Code - https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code - AI coding agent that works in your terminal to help you code faster through natural language

That's all for today.

Michal Nezerka
michal.nezerka@sap.com

entry.title: The Joule Effect: Event Recap ⚡️
entry.content:
Last week at the SAP Casablanca office, we brought together SAP professionals and enthusiasts for our event The Joule Effect, a focused session on the future of Business AI.

With the support of the SAP Community team, and contributions from @Salah2  and @belahmew, we explored what SAP Joule really means for our ecosystem.

Key Takeaways

  • AI is changing the game: Faster, simpler SAP interactions
  • Clean Core is essential: The foundation for scalable and secure AI adoption
  • Consultants remain key: Technology evolves, but expertise drives real transformation

Beyond the Content

What stood out most was the exchange between experienced professionals and emerging SAP talents. This is exactly what we want to build in Casablanca: a space to learn, connect, and grow together.

What’s Next

This is just the start. Our goal is to establish a consistent SAP knowledge hub in Morocco.

If you missed it:

  • Follow the Casablanca tag on SAP Community
  • Connect with us on LinkedIn
  • Share what topic we should cover next

Thank you to everyone who made this first step a success. More is coming.

entry.title: Germany eInvoicing Mandate: Technical Pitfalls for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud — and How to Avoid Them
entry.content:
Introduction

Germany is in the final stretch of a major regulatory shift in business-to-business invoicing. The legal foundation — the Wachstumschancengesetz and the amended §14 UStG — together with an EU derogation for mandatory B2B e-invoicing, sets out a clear and binding timeline:

Date Obligation

1 January 2025All German businesses must be able to receive EN 16931-compliant e-invoices (at least one of the following: XRechnung, ZUGFeRD 2.x, Peppol BIS 3.0)
Until 31 December 2026Paper and PDF may still be issued with the recipient's consent; reception duty already applies
1 January 2027Mandatory issuance of structured e-invoices for businesses whose prior-year revenue exceeded 800.000 EUR
1 January 2028Issuance obligation extends to all domestic B2B businesses, regardless of turnover

Only structured, machine-readable formats aligned with EN 16931 qualify as valid e-invoices. Pure PDFs — even sent by e-mail — and non-EN 16931 EDI formats no longer meet the legal requirement. This is also true for the good old INVOIC02 IDoc, by the way. The mandate applies to invoices exchanged between businesses VAT-established in Germany (Inland-zu-Inland).

For organisations running SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud or On-Premise, 2026 is the preparation year. The following four technical go-live pitfalls are among the most frequently encountered when setting up the SAP eDocument framework for Germany. Knowing them in advance can save your project significant time and frustration.

Before You Start: Align Finance, Tax, and IT

Technical configuration alone will not get you to a successful go-live. E-invoicing projects regularly stall or require expensive rework because the three key functions — Finance, Tax, and IT — operate in silos.

  • Finance owns the invoice lifecycle, approval workflows, and supplier relationships. They feel the pain first when processes break.
  • Tax carries the compliance responsibility. If mandatory fields such as VAT rates, buyer/seller data, or country-specific codes are missing, the invoice is legally invalid — regardless of whether the XML was transmitted successfully.
  • IT builds and operates the integration. But without clear, early requirements from Finance and Tax, they will build the wrong thing on time.

The pattern repeats across implementations: IT is halfway through the SAP eDocument setup, Tax steps in and discovers that several legally required fields are not being populated, and the project is delayed by weeks while mappings are redone and data models are extended. This is not a technology failure — it is a coordination failure.

What the best-prepared projects do differently:

  1. Form a joint steering group with representatives from all three functions from day one.
  2. Co-create requirements: map the full invoice lifecycle together, agree on which fields are legally mandatory versus operationally useful, and identify where master data gaps exist before configuration begins.
  3. Define shared success criteria: invoice validation rate, audit readiness, and mandate coverage — not just "went live on time."

For a deeper look at this cross-functional dynamic and how to build your organisation's e-invoicing team, see Fabio Santoro's article The E-Invoicing Dream Team: Why Finance, Tax, and IT Must Play as One.

With that organizational groundwork in place, the you are good to go and navigate the technical implementation. The pitfalls are outlined in this Blog Post.

Pitfall 1 — eDocument Is Never Created

Symptom

An SD billing document or FI invoice has been posted successfully, but the eDocument Cockpit (EDOC_COCKPIT) shows nothing. No eDocument appears, and nothing can be submitted.

Root Causes and Fixes

1.1 Missing Customizing — Assign eDocument Types

The eDocument framework must know which document types should produce an eDocument.

T003EDOCV — Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type

This view is evaluated when the eDocument is created from the FI accounting document path (source type FI_INVOICE) or SD Billing document path (source type SD_INVOICE). It maps the accounting document type (e.g. DR customer invoice, DG credit memo) to an eDocument type. This covers:

  • Accounting documents posted directly in FI (e.g. transaction FB01)
  • The FI document created by the VF01 billing run, when the eDocument framework is also triggered from the FI event

Navigate to Customizing → Cross-Application Components → General Application Functions → Document and Reporting Compliance → Country/Region-Specific Settings → Germany → Electronic Document Processing → General Settings → Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type.

Decision at a glance

Trigger path Source type in framework View evaluated

SD billing document (VF01) with Accounting DocumentSD_INVOICET003EDOCV
FI accounting document (FB01 or FI transfer from SD)FI_INVOICET003EDOCV
Pro forma / billing type without accounting docSD_INVOICEnot supported for Peppol solution / ZUGFeRD

💡Note for S/4HANA Public Cloud (Cloud Edition) readers

  • TVFKEDOCV is the only relevant view for SD Billing Documents in Public Cloud.
  • T003EDOCV is irrelevant for the SD_INVOICE source path in Public Cloud — only the FI_INVOICE path still uses it.

If you are running Public Cloud and your SD Billing Documents are not generating eDocuments, check TVFKEDOCV first.

💡Practical guidance for Germany (Private Cloud / On-Premise): For the typical German B2B invoice triggered via the SD billing flow (VF01) or direct FI postings (FB01), maintain T003EDOCV.

➡️Reference: Assigning eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type | SAP Help Portal

1.2 Source Document Types Not Activated for the Company Code

After defining the eDocument types, the Source Document Types must be activated per company code. Without this step, no eDocument will be created even if the type assignment is correct.

➡️Reference: Activating Source Type Documents for Company Code | SAP Help Portal

1.3 Interface Type Not Defined for the eDocument

Each eDocument type requires an interface type definition that determines how the document is transmitted. For Germany with Peppol Network Exchange, this points to the appropriate SOA service and logical port.

➡️Reference: Defining Interface Type (Peppol) | SAP Help Portal

➡️Reference: Defining Interface Type (ZUGFeRD) | SAP Help Portal

1.4 Customer Not Maintained in EDOEUBUPAV

The most common single cause of missing eDocuments: the customer of the invoice has not been registered in the business partner Peppol registration view.

  • Execute transaction SM30, enter view EDOEUBUPAV, and click Maintain.
  • Add the customer number, their Peppol participant identifier type (e.g. VAT number, Leitweg-ID), and the identifier value.
  • Without this entry, the system cannot determine the Peppol recipient and will not create the eDocument.

➡️Reference: Maintaining Organisation Registration Number for Business Partners | SAP Help Portal

💡Productivity Tip — Discover Peppol-Registered Business Partners Automatically

Rather than emailing each business partner to ask for their Peppol ID, use the SAP-delivered report "List Business Partners for Peppol Exchange of Electronic Documents" (transaction EDOEUBUPAV-lookup, implemented via SAP Note 3350153).

The report scans your business partner master data (VAT numbers, Leitweg-IDs, KVK numbers, etc.), matches them against the live Peppol network directory, and returns a list showing which partners are already registered. Business partners that are Peppol participants but not yet enabled in your system can be activated with a single click — no manual data collection needed.

➡️Implement via: SAP Note 3350153

1.5 Retrigger eDocument Creation

Once customizing is corrected, previously missed invoices can be processed retroactively:

  1. Execute EDOC_COCKPIT.
  2. Next to Variant, click Change.
  3. Select the appropriate tab (e.g. Billing Documents for SD Billing Documents).
  4. Check "Not yet created".
  5. Execute the search. In the results, identify entries marked as Relevant.
  6. Select them and click Create.

Pitfall 2 — Outbound Communication Failures (HTTP 401, 404, or Wrong Logical Port)

Symptom

When submitting an eDocument, the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents Fiori app returns errors such as:

  • HTTP Code 401: Unauthorized
  • HTTP Code 404: Not Found
  • SOAP:1007 SRT: Unsupported xstream found
  • A logon popup appears unexpectedly

Root Causes and Fixes

2.1 SM30 → EDOSOASERV: The Bridge Between eDocument and SOAMANAGER

View EDOSOASERV is the critical link that tells the eDocument framework which SOAMANAGER logical port to call for each service. It is maintained via SM30 or via Customizing → Cross-Application Components → General Application Functions → Document and Reporting Compliance → Country/Region-Specific Settings → Germany → Electronic Document Processing → General Settings → Define SOA Services for Communication.

Required entries for Germany (Peppol / XRechnung outbound):

SOA Service Name Logical Port Purpose

EU_INVOICE_TRANSMEDO_UBL_SEND_INVOICE_SERV_PORTOutbound invoice submission
EU_CR_NOTE_TRANSMEDO_UBL_SEND_CRED_NOTE_SERV_PORTOutbound credit note submission
EU_APP_RESP_TRANSMEDO_UBL_APPLICATION_RESPONSEOutgoing invoice response to business partner
EU_GENERIC_WEBSERVEDO_EU_GENERIC_WEBSERVHTML display (eDocument Cockpit / Manage Electronic Documents)
EU_EMAIL_PREPAREEDO_EU_GENERIC_WEBSERVCross-Industry-Invoice validation validation (Customer Invoice via E-Mail)

Important rules:

  • If the Company Code field is left empty, the entry acts as the default for all company codes not covered by a more specific entry.
  • If a specific company code is entered, the entry applies only to that company code.
  • The logical port name here must exactly match the name configured in SOAMANAGER
  • The corresponding logical port in SOAMANAGER must point to the correct proxy object (e.g. CO_EDO_UBL_SEND_INVOICE_V1_0 for invoice submission, CO_DCS_DCP_GENERIC_WEBSERVICE for HTML display).

➡️Reference: Defining SOA Services for Electronic Customer Invoices| SAP Help Portal

➡️Reference: Defining SOA Services for Electronic Customer Invoices (Submission by E-Mail)| SAP Help Portal

➡️Reference: Creating Logical Ports in SOA Management | SAP Help Portal

2.3 Wrong URL Path in the Logical Port

The URL path in each logical port must point to the correct environment endpoint:

Environment Path

Test/cxf/PeppolSendDocumentTest
Production/cxf/PeppolSendDocumentProd

Other settings: Protocol = HTTPS, Port = 443, URL type = URL components.

2.4 Incorrect Authentication

S/4HANA Private Cloud / On-Premise supports two authentication methods against SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition:

  • X.509 Client Certificate: The client certificate must be signed by a Certificate Authority listed in SAP Note 2801396. Export the certificate from transaction STRUST (without private key, PEM format, no whitespace), and paste it into the service binding in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

  • OAuth 2.0 (recommended): OAuth 2.0 credentials do not expire and do not require a client certificate signed by a specific trusted CA. Configure via transaction OA2C_CONFIG. The OAuth client ID, secret, and token endpoint URL are obtained from the service binding in SAP BTP.

    ➡️Reference: OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication | SAP Help Portal

Tip: If you are starting fresh, prefer OAuth 2.0. Certificates expire and require renewal; OAuth 2.0 credentials do not.

2.5 Testing the Connection

In SOAMANAGER, use Ping Web Service on your logical port. A response of:

  • HTTP 405 (Method Not Allowed) = connection is working (the service does not support HTTP HEAD, but the network path is reachable)
  • HTTP 200 (OK) = connection is working
  • HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) = authentication misconfiguration → review Section 2.4
  • SSL handshake error = trust issue → see Pitfall 3

Pitfall 3 — SSL Handshake Fails: SSSLERR_PEER_CERT_UNTRUSTED

Symptom

When submitting an eDocument or pinging the web service, the system throws an error like:

SSL handshake with ...:443 failed: SSSLERR_PEER_CERT_UNTRUSTED (-102)
Peer's X.509 certificate (chain) validation failed (missing trust?)

Root Cause

The server's root certificate — the topmost certificate in the chain presented by the SAP DRC service endpoint — has not been imported into the certificate trust list of the SAP system's PSE in transaction STRUST.

Solution

Step 1 — Retrieve the root certificate via your browser

  1. Open the service binding URL (e.g. https://peppol-cf-cust.it-cpi018-rt.cfapps.eu10-003.hana.ondemand.com) in a web browser.
  2. Click the padlock icon in the address bar → View Certificate (or Certificate Information).
  3. Navigate to the Certification Path tab.
  4. Select the topmost (root) certificate in the chain.
  5. Export / download it to your local machine.

Step 2 — Import the root certificate into STRUST

  1. Execute transaction STRUST.
  2. Select the PSE used by the logical port (typically SSL Client (Standard)).
  3. Click Display.
  4. Click Import Certificate and select the downloaded root certificate file.
  5. Click Add to Certificate List.
  6. Save.

Once the root CA is trusted, SAP will trust all certificates issued by that authority — you do not need to import intermediate certificates separately.

For OAuth 2.0 users: also apply the same steps for the token endpoint host (typically https://peppol-cf-cust.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com), which may use a different certificate chain.

For client certificate users: ensure your client certificate is issued by a CA from SAP Note 2801396. The SAP BTP side validates the client certificate against this list.

➡️Reference: Adding Server Root Certificate to Certificate Trust List | SAP Help Portal

Pitfall 4 — HTML Display Fails in eDocument Cockpit

Symptom

After creating an eDocument, clicking Display HTML in EDOC_COCKPIT produces:

No Interface is defined for process step DISPL_HTML (Message no. EDOCUMENT034)

Or the display call fails silently / returns an authentication error.

Root Causes and Fixes

4.1 Missing Logical Port for HTML Display Service

A separate logical port is required for the HTML rendering service. Create logical port EDO_EU_GENERIC_WEBSERV in SOAMANAGER for the service CO_DCS_DCP_GENERIC_WEBSERVICE.

➡️Reference: Defining SOA Services for Communication | SAP Help Portal

4.2 Authentication Mismatch (Test vs. Production)

The service binding and the credentials configured in SOAMANAGER / OA2C_CONFIG must correspond to the same environment (test or production). Mixing test credentials with a production URL — or vice versa — results in authentication failures that manifest as display errors.

Checklist:

  • Service binding URL in the logical port → matches the intended environment?
  • OAuth 2.0 client credentials in OA2C_CONFIG → sourced from the same service binding?
  • Client certificate in STRUST → exported from the correct service binding?

Pitfall 5 — eDocument Stuck at "Received by Sender Access Point" (Yellow Indicator)

Symptom

After an invoice is submitted to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition (via Peppol or E-Mail scenario), the eDocument Cockpit shows the status "Received by sender access point" or "Request Acknowledged" — indicated by a yellow triangle — and the document does not progress further. A corresponding red entry may appear in the Document Monitor (Peppol Exchange app on BTP).

This means the document was received and queued by SAP DRC in the cloud, but the push-back response to the SAP backend could not be delivered - either because the inbound channel from BTP to your S/4HANA system is misconfigured, or because the backend cannot correctly process the incoming response.

Root Causes and Fixes

5.1 Cloud Connector Not Reachable or Not Connected (HTTP 503)

Error pattern:

Server returned HTTP response code: 503 for URL: http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/srt/rfc/sap/dcppushservice/.../dcppushservice/pushrequest
Error Stream: There is no SAP Cloud Connector (SCC) connected to your subaccount matching the requested tunnel ...

or:

Timed out waiting for tunnel to open for tunnelId account:///...

Cause: The SAP Cloud Connector is not running, not connected to the BTP subaccount, or the tunnel for the backend system has not been established.

Resolution:

  1. Verify that the SAP Cloud Connector is running and connected: in the BTP Cockpit, under Connectivity → Cloud Connectors, the Cloud Connector should appear as Connected.
  2. Review the Cloud Connector subaccount configuration: ➡️ Creating Subaccount in Cloud Connector | SAP Help Portal ➡️ Cloud Connector | SAP Help Portal
  3. Verify the resource mapping (the backend URL path must be exposed in the Cloud Connector): ➡️ Adding Resource to Connection | SAP Help Portal
  4. Check whether the Cloud Connector's subaccount certificate has expired: ➡️ SAP Note 2950537 — Subaccount certificate expired in SAP Cloud Connector
  5. Also review firewall settings — the Cloud Connector must be able to reach the BTP endpoint outbound.
  6. Confirm the BTP Destination connection test passes in the Destinations app in BTP.

5.2 Company Code Cannot Be Determined for the Peppol Participant

Error pattern (English):

ERROR: Determination of the company code for participants <scheme>:<id> not possible

Error pattern (German):

Ermittlung des Buchungskreises für Beteiligten <scheme>:<id> nicht möglich

Cause: The good thing is - if you see this message, the connection was successful. The bad thing is - when SAP DRC pushes the an incoming Invoice back, the backend must map the Peppol Sender Participant ID to a company code. If this mapping is ambiguous or missing, the push response will be rejected.

Resolution — step by step:

  1. Identify the participant identifier scheme. Check whether the prefix corresponds to a VAT number (e.g. scheme 9930 is a German VAT ID; see the Peppol Participant Identifier Schemes list).

  2. If the scheme is a VAT number (e.g. 9930):

    • Open transaction OBY6 (company code global settings) and verify that exactly one company code is linked to the VAT number shown in the Sender Participant ID.
    • Also check table T001, field STCEG in SE16 for duplicate VAT entries across company codes.
    • If multiple company codes share the same VAT number, consider switching to GLN-based Peppol IDs to avoid ambiguity. ➡️ Background: Peppol IDs in the context of SAP Document Compliance | SAP Blogs

  3. If the scheme is not a VAT number (e.g. GLN or other):

    • Check table T001Z, field PAVAL in SE16 for duplicate company code parameter entries.
    • Again, duplicates here prevent unambiguous mapping.
  4. Custom extension for Company Code determination
    • If none of the above is feasible, consider a customer implementation.

5.3 Provider Service Not Configured or Misconfigured (HTTP 500 / 401 / 403)

Error patterns:

Server returned HTTP response code: 500 ...
"No Web service configuration for this access path: /sap/bc/srt/rfc/sap/dcppushservice/.../dcppushservice/pushrequest"

or HTTP 401 on the push URL, or:

Server returned HTTP response code: 403 ...
Access denied to resource / on system <host>:<port>. In case this was a valid request, ensure to expose the resource correctly in your cloud connector.

(Note: when only the host is shown in the error — without a path — the full URL path is missing from the BTP Destination.)

Cause: The SAP backend was reached, but the inbound SOAP web service for receiving push responses (dcppushservice) has not been activated or is misconfigured.

Resolution:

  1. Configure the inbound provider service in the SAP backend: ➡️ Configuring Provider Service | SAP Help Portal
  2. Verify the transport protocol setting: the protocol configured in the provider service (HTTP vs. HTTPS) must match the Transport Level Security setting in the Cloud Connector resource mapping. They must be in sync.
  3. Verify the BTP Destination URL includes the full path to the provider service — not just the hostname. A missing path causes 403 errors.
  4. Check the communication user's credentials and ensure the user account is not locked: ➡️ Setting Up Connectivity | SAP Help Portal

5.4 BTP Destination URL Has No Path (HTTP Reaches Host Only)

Error pattern:

java.io.IOException: http://<host>:443 | Error Stream: Service cannot be reached

Cause: The BTP Destination is configured with only the hostname, without a path to the provider web service.

Resolution: Update the BTP Destination URL to include the full path to the dcppushservice provider service as documented: ➡️ Adding Resource to Connection | SAP Help Portal

Rule: Always provide a valid path to the web service endpoint in the BTP Destination URL field, not just the host. Refer to the Setting Up Connectivity | SAP Help Portal for the exact URL structure.

5.5 Authentication Error on the Push URL (HTTP 401)

Error pattern:

Server returned HTTP response code: 401 for URL: http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/srt/rfc/sap/dcppushservice/...

Cause: The communication user configured in the BTP Destination has incorrect credentials or is locked.

Resolution:

  1. Verify the username and password in the BTP Destination match a valid, unlocked SAP backend user.
  2. Test the user credentials directly by calling http://<internal-host>/sap/bc/ping?sap-client=<client> from a browser inside your network — authentication should succeed.
  3. Verify the full provider service URL is also reachable with those credentials.

5.6 Destination ID Exceeds 10-Character Limit

Error pattern (visible in the Peppol Document Monitor):

"Peppol_Backend_Connection-S4" contravenes the maxLength facet "10"

Cause: The BTP Destination name used to identify the backend system is longer than the 10-character maximum enforced by DRC.

Resolution: Rename the BTP Destination to a value with 10 characters or fewer, and update all references accordingly. ➡️ Setting Up Connectivity | S/4HANA or ERP

5.7 No Error Shown — Push Response Received but Not Processed

If no error is visible in the eDocument Cockpit and the yellow status persists, the push response may have reached the SAP backend but stalled in the inbound message queue.

Check:

  1. Execute transaction EDOC_INBOUND_MSG.
  2. Search for pending messages using List Existing Messages.
  3. Set Message Type to DE_PUSH_RESPONSE (for outgoing Invices via Peppol) or DE_BUSLVLRESP_PUSH (for incoming Application Responses via Peppol) or DE_CII_PUSH (for E-Mail scenarios) or DE_MESSAGE_PUSH (for incoming Invoices).
  4. Process any pending messages to advance the eDocument status.
  5. If transaction EDOC_INBOUND_MSG shows no messages at all - verify your Participant's Destination and make sure it points to your S/4HANA BTP Destination.

Summary Checklist

Use this quick-reference table before and during go-live preparation:

# Symptom Most Likely Cause Key Action

1aeDocument not created — no entry in EDOC_COCKPITMissing T003EDOCV customizingAssign eDocument Type to document types; activate per company code
1beDocument not created — no entry in EDOC_COCKPITCustomer missing in EDOEUBUPAVMaintain Peppol ID in SM30 → EDOEUBUPAV; use Note 3350153 report
2aHTTP 401 on submissionWrong authentication setupConfigure OAuth 2.0 or X.509 client cert correctly in SOAMANAGER
2bHTTP 404 or wrong endpointIncorrect logical port name or URL pathVerify logical port names and paths against SAP Help documentation
3SSSLERR_PEER_CERT_UNTRUSTED on any callServer root cert not trusted in STRUSTDownload root cert from browser; import into STRUST PSE trust list
4HTML display authentication errorMissing / wrong logical port for CO_DCS_DCP_GENERIC_WEBSERVICECreate EDO_EU_GENERIC_WEBSERV in SOAMANAGER
5aYellow status "Received by sender access point"Cloud Connector not connected or firewall blockingVerify Cloud Connector status; check BTP Destination connection test
5bYellow status + company code error in logAmbiguous Peppol participant ID → company code mappingCheck OBY6 / T001 / T001Z for duplicates
5cYellow status + HTTP 500 or 403 on push URLProvider service not configured or path missing in DestinationFollow Configuring Provider Service guide; add full path to BTP Destination
5dYellow status + HTTP 401 on push URLCommunication user locked or wrong credentialsVerify user in BTP Destination; test with /sap/bc/ping
5eDestination name too long error in cloud logBTP Destination name > 10 charactersRename Destination to ≤ 10 characters

Closing Thoughts

The German eInvoicing mandate is real, the deadlines are fixed, and the technical setup on SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud involves several layers — customizing, SOA connectivity, certificate management, business partner maintenance, and the inbound push-response channel — that must all be correct simultaneously. The pitfalls described above account for a large share of the support cases seen in practice.

The earlier you start, the more time you have to discover and address your specific configuration gaps. And with tools like the Peppol business partner lookup report (SAP Note 3350153), even the time-consuming task of identifying your Peppol-capable customer base can be automated.

entry.title: How SAP's AI copilot is moving from helper to agentic layer that reasons and acts inside HR workflow
entry.content:
Your organization likely spent the last two years deploying conversational AI as a convenience layer: a chat window that answers leave balance questions and helps employees find the right form. Useful. Incremental. Already obsolete.

The shift underway is not from chatbot to better chatbot. It is from tool to colleague. SAP's Joule is moving from a system that responds to one that reasons, plans, and acts, often before anyone asks it to. The implications for HR operating models are far more disruptive than most organizations have internalized.

Deloitte's State of AI in the Enterprise 2026 report found that nearly three-quarters of companies plan to deploy agentic AI within two years. Yet only 21% report having a mature governance model for autonomous agents.[1] That gap between ambition and readiness is where the real risk sits.

This piece examines what agentic AI actually means for HR, how Joule's architecture is evolving to deliver it, and what organizations need to get right before agents start making decisions alongside their people.

The shift from copilot to co-worker

The terminology matters, because the technology has outgrown the language most organizations use to describe it.

A chatbot retrieves information. You ask a question; it returns an answer. The interaction is bounded, reactive, and stateless.

A copilot augments human action. It drafts, summarizes, recommends. It operates within a single workflow at a user's request. Most of what organizations call "AI in HR" today lives here.

An agent does something categorically different. It reasons over a goal, decomposes it into steps, gathers information from multiple systems, makes decisions within defined guardrails, and takes action, often across process boundaries. Critically, it retains context, learns from outcomes, and can initiate work without being prompted.

SAP announced Joule in September 2023 as a generative AI copilot.[2] In the roughly two and a half years since, it has evolved into an agentic platform. The distinction is not academic. When Joule shifts from answering "What is my leave balance?" to proactively telling a manager "Three of your five critical successors have development plans that are stalled. Here's what I recommend," that is not a better chatbot. That is a new kind of organizational actor.

What makes agentic AI different

Deloitte's Tech Trends 2026 report frames the distinction sharply: organizations must begin treating AI agents as a "silicon-based workforce" that complements the human workforce, with dedicated management frameworks for onboarding, performance tracking, and cost management.[3] The metaphor is intentional. Agents are not features to be toggled on. They are workers that happen to run on silicon, and they require governance, oversight, and integration into team structures just as any new hire would.

Most organizations remain early in this transition. While 30% are exploring agentic options and 38% are piloting solutions, only 14% have solutions ready for deployment, and a mere 11% are actively using these systems in production.[4] Legacy system integration, data architecture constraints, and the absence of enterprise-grade governance remain the primary barriers.

For HR, the distinction between copilot and agent carries operational weight. A copilot helps a manager draft a performance review. An agent gathers the employee's goal progress, recent feedback, learning completions, and skills data, synthesizes it into talking points, and suggests follow-up actions like updating a development goal or scheduling a coaching conversation. The manager's role shifts from data assembly to decision-making.

Joule's architecture: why embedded matters

Most enterprise AI copilots sit on top of business applications. They access data through APIs, generate responses in a separate interface, and operate at arm's length from the processes they are meant to improve. This architecture creates a fundamental ceiling: the AI can inform decisions but cannot participate in them.

Joule's architecture is different in a way that matters operationally. It is embedded directly into SAP Business Suite, not bolted on, but woven into the transaction layer of SuccessFactors, S/4HANA, Ariba, Concur, and SAP Analytics Cloud.[5] This means Joule operates within the same security model, the same role-based permissions, and the same workflow logic that governs every human user.

This design choice addresses a critical challenge: nearly half of organizations cite data searchability (48%) and data reusability (47%) as obstacles to their AI automation strategy.[4] By operating within SAP's data layer rather than above it, Joule avoids the integration bottleneck that hampers many enterprise AI deployments.

SAP states that Joule now supports 80% of the most-used tasks across SAP systems, is available in 11 languages, and integrates with Microsoft 365 Copilot, allowing users to access SAP intelligence directly from Outlook and Teams without navigating to SuccessFactors.[6] The Microsoft integration signals that agents are no longer confined to the system they were built in.

The integration with SAP's existing access controls is worth emphasizing. An AI that recommends a successor from outside your permissions context is a liability. An AI that respects the same role-based permission structure as your HRBP, seeing only what that role should see, acting only within those boundaries, is something an organization can actually govern.

The agent roster: what Joule does in HR today

It is easy to dismiss agents as vaporware if you only read roadmap slides. Here is what has shipped and what has firm delivery dates.

Performance and Goals Agent (generally available, November 2025). This was the first Joule Agent purpose-built for HR.[7] Rather than waiting for a manager to ask for performance data, the agent proactively gathers goals, achievements, feedback, and review history for each direct report. It synthesizes this into personalized discussion points and suggests follow-up actions: schedule a one-on-one, update a development goal, adjust a rating based on new evidence. The agent transforms performance conversations from administrative exercises into strategic coaching moments.

Four additional agents (expected general availability, May 2026):[8]

The Career and Talent Development Agent automates succession planning and helps managers identify future leaders. It reasons over existing succession plans, prompts managers for updates when plans go stale, recommends successors based on skills and experience data, and generates personalized development plans. The operative word is "reasons", this is not a report. It is an agent that evaluates the quality of your succession pipeline and acts when it finds gaps.

The HR Service Agent functions as a first line of response for employee questions about company policies, benefits, and processes. Before routing a question to a human, it searches company policy documents and knowledge bases. If it cannot resolve the query, it raises a ticket in the SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Service Center.

The People Intelligence Agent connects workforce analytics in SAP Business Data Cloud with Joule's conversational interface. HR leaders can ask questions in natural language, about attrition risk, skills distribution, or capability gaps, and receive visual analytics with recommendations, not just data tables.

The Payroll Agent addresses one of the most common sources of employee frustration: understanding pay. Building on SAP's existing Explain Pay feature in Joule, which integrates data from SuccessFactors time tracking and reportedly reduces pay-related resolution time by up to 50%, the Payroll Agent extends this capability to both employees and payroll administrators, providing natural-language explanations for pay changes connected to specific time entries like overtime, absences, or shift differentials.[9]

At SAP Connect 2025, SAP announced more than a dozen new Joule Agents spanning finance, procurement, supply chain, and HR, part of a broader expansion that brought the total to more than 40 agents across SAP's portfolio.[10] The pattern across all of them is consistent: each agent is domain-specific, operates within enterprise security boundaries, and is designed to reason and act, not just respond.

Additionally, SAP has introduced a workforce knowledge network that integrates third-party content from industry experts, including G-P (Globalization Partners) and The Josh Bersin Company, directly into Joule, enabling agent-to-agent interaction that grounds recommendations in external research and best practices.[11]

Joule Studio: building custom agents

Ready-made agents address common workflows. But every organization has processes that are unique, and this is where Joule Studio enters the picture.

Joule Studio, a capability within SAP Build, reached general availability for skill building in July 2025 and for agent building in January 2026.[12] It allows organizations to create custom Joule agents and skills using a low-code interface, grounded in SAP's Knowledge Graph and business data.

Two capabilities make Joule Studio strategically significant.

First, Model Context Protocol (MCP) support allows custom agents to connect with external tools and non-SAP systems through an open standard.[13] This is not a proprietary integration framework. An HR agent built in Joule Studio can pull compensation benchmarking data from a third-party system and combine it with internal SuccessFactors data to make a recommendation, breaking down the data silos that consistently impede AI value.

Second, multi-agent orchestration allows agents to function as tools for one another. Joule can invoke a recruiting agent and a workforce planning agent in sequence to solve a problem that spans both domains, identifying a skills gap, finding internal candidates, and flagging external hiring needs in a single workflow.[14] This coordination is what distinguishes an agent ecosystem from a collection of disconnected tools.

Early adopters are building production-grade agents quickly. At SAP's Hack2Build event in January 2026, partner teams, including Capgemini, which described Joule Studio as enabling connectivity across SAP and non-SAP environments in hybrid landscapes, created functional custom agents in under seven days.[15]

The governance gap

The capability is moving fast. The governance is not.

The data on this point is unambiguous. While 74% of companies expect to use agentic AI within two years, only 21% have a mature model for governance of autonomous agents.[1] The risks that concern organizations most all relate to governance: data privacy and security (73%), legal and regulatory compliance (50%), and governance capabilities and oversight (46%).[1]

For HR, the governance challenge is acute. AI agents in HR make decisions that affect people's careers, compensation, development opportunities, and access to roles. An agent that recommends successors shapes who gets developed. An agent that answers policy questions defines how policy is interpreted. An agent that explains pay determines whether employees trust their employer.

These are not edge cases. They are the core of what HR does.

A minimum viable governance model for agentic AI in HR should address several critical dimensions:

Permissions inheritance. Role-based permissions must extend cleanly to agents, if a manager cannot see certain employee data, their agent cannot either. SAP has built this into Joule's architecture through principal propagation, meaning the user's identity is passed end-to-end so that any agent action runs within that user's security context.[5]

Human-in-the-loop checkpoints. High-stakes decisions, succession nominations, compensation recommendations, talent mobility, require defined approval points. Joule Studio enables human-in-the-loop by default.[12]

Auditability. Organizations must be able to explain why an agent made a specific recommendation. This is particularly important as regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act impose transparency requirements on high-risk AI systems.

Bias monitoring. When agents influence talent decisions at scale, bias monitoring must be operationalized, not just discussed. This is especially critical for agents involved in succession planning, career development, and internal mobility recommendations.

Platform guardrails are necessary but not sufficient. The harder work is organizational: defining which decisions agents can make autonomously, which require human approval, and how to measure whether agents are making those decisions fairly.

The economic case

The investment case for agentic AI in HR is strengthening, but executives should approach the numbers with appropriate nuance.

SAP's Value of AI Report, conducted with Oxford Economics in 2025, found that organizations are seeing an average 16% ROI on AI investments (approximately $4.7 million), with expectations for that to nearly double to 31% within two years. Seventy-nine percent expect positive ROI in less than three years.[16]

However, Deloitte's research provides important counterweight. Most organizations achieve satisfactory ROI on AI use cases within two to four years, significantly longer than the seven-to-twelve–month payback period typically expected for technology investments.[17] And 65% of respondents in SAP's own study acknowledge they are unsure or do not believe AI is delivering its full potential, even as they report positive returns.[16]

The reconciliation is straightforward. AI delivers measurable productivity gains on narrow use cases, drafting, summarization, Q&A automation, relatively quickly. The transformative impact, redesigning processes, redeploying talent, making better strategic decisions, takes longer and requires organizational change that most companies are still building.

For HR leaders, the practical implication is a dual-speed strategy. Capture quick wins with copilot-level capabilities (Joule answering employee queries, drafting performance reviews, explaining pay) while investing in the data quality, governance, and change management required for agent-level capabilities (automated succession planning, skills-based talent recommendations, workforce analytics on demand).

What this means for the HR operating model

The deeper question is not whether to deploy Joule agents, but what happens to HR when agents handle an increasing share of operational work.

Deloitte and ServiceNow's 2026 enterprise research describes a future where employees increasingly serve as "orchestrators and supervisors of AI agents rather than direct executors of tasks."[18] For HR, this implies a structural shift in how the function allocates its capacity.

Consider the HR Service Center. Today, a significant portion of HR service delivery involves answering repetitive questions, leave policies, benefits eligibility, payroll explanations, onboarding logistics. If an HR Service Agent handles a majority of these queries autonomously, the freed capacity creates space for HR professionals to focus on strategic workforce planning, manager coaching, organizational design, and the contextual employee support that no agent can replicate.

SAP itself is modeling this internally. Its People & Culture Lounge concept, announced in late 2025, provides all 110,000 SAP employees access to one-on-one conversations with HR experts. The rationale is explicit: because technologies like Joule resolve standard inquiries efficiently, personal conversation in complex situations becomes more important, not less.[19]

The pattern is instructive: agents handle volume. Humans handle judgment. The organizations that get the division of labor right will have HR functions that are both more efficient and more human, not one at the expense of the other.

A practical path forward

For SuccessFactors customers evaluating Joule's agentic capabilities, the path forward requires sequencing technology, data, and governance in the right order.

Start with data readiness, not agent deployment. Every agent is only as good as the data it reasons over. If your job architecture is inconsistent, your skills taxonomy is unmaintained, or your performance data is sparse, agents will produce unreliable recommendations, and users will lose trust before the technology gets a chance to prove itself. Clean your Talent Intelligence Hub data. Ensure Growth Portfolios are populated. Validate that role-based permissions are correctly configured.

Activate Joule and capture copilot-level value now. Joule's conversational capabilities, answering employee questions, drafting content, navigating SuccessFactors, are available today and deliver immediate productivity returns. Build user familiarity and organizational comfort with AI in HR workflows before introducing agents that act autonomously.

Deploy the Performance and Goals Agent as your first agent. It is generally available, scoped to a well-understood process, and delivers value that managers will notice immediately. Use it to establish governance patterns, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, feedback mechanisms, monitoring protocols, that you can extend to more complex agents later.

Build governance before you need it. Define which talent decisions require human approval. Establish auditability standards for agent recommendations. Create bias monitoring protocols for agents that influence succession, development, and career pathing. The May 2026 wave of agents will arrive whether you are ready or not.

Plan for Joule Studio when ready-made agents are not enough. If your organization has unique workflows, custom onboarding sequences, industry-specific compliance processes, bespoke talent review cycles, Joule Studio allows you to create custom agents grounded in your business data. But this is a Phase 2 capability. Get the fundamentals right first.

Where to start

The trajectory is unmistakable. Joule is evolving from a conversational interface into an agentic layer that will increasingly participate in, not just support, HR decision-making. The Performance and Goals Agent is the first tangible proof point. The Career and Talent Development, HR Service, People Intelligence, and Payroll agents arriving in 2026 will extend this pattern across the HR lifecycle.

But the technology is ahead of most organizations' readiness. The data is clear: agentic AI governance lags adoption by a wide margin. The organizations that invest now in data quality, permissions architecture, and governance frameworks will be positioned to capture value from agents as they mature. Those that wait for agents to force the issue will spend their time managing risk instead of creating value.

Joule is not a chatbot. Increasingly, it is not even a copilot. It is becoming a new category of HR participant, one that reasons, acts, and learns within the same systems your people use every day. The question for HR leaders is not whether to engage with this shift. It is whether they will shape it, or be shaped by it.

What is your organization's readiness for agentic AI in HR? I'd welcome a conversation, particularly from those who have deployed the Performance and Goals Agent or are planning for the May 2026 wave.

References

 

  1. Deloitte, State of AI in the Enterprise, 2026 Edition
  2. SAP, SAP Announces New Generative AI Assistant Joule
  3. Deloitte, Tech Trends 2026
  4. Deloitte, Agentic AI Strategy, Tech Trends 2026
  5. SAP, Joule Artificial Intelligence Assistant
  6. SAP, What's New with SAP Business AI, Joule, and Joule Agents in the SAP SuccessFactors 2H 2025 Release
  7. SAP, Innovation Guide H2 2025
  8. SAP, New Joule Agents and Embedded Intelligence Supercharge Business Returns
  9. SAP, Agent Builder in Joule Studio Is Now Generally Available
  10. SAP, Build Business-Grounded AI Agents, Faster
  11. SAP, Unprecedented Possibilities: New SAP SuccessFactors Innovations
  12. SAP, Joule Studio
  13. SAP, Build Business-Grounded AI Agents, Faster
  14. SAP, Partner-Driven Innovation Takes Off with Agent Builder in Joule Studio
  15. SAP and Oxford Economics, The Value of AI Report
  16. Deloitte, AI ROI: The Paradox of Rising Investment and Elusive Returns
  17. Deloitte and ServiceNow, The AI-Fueled Enterprise of 2026
  18. SAP, More Than Just Technology: Why Personal HR Support Gains Importance in the Age of AI

entry.title: Default Segmentation Determination in documents (Blog 2)
entry.content:
In this blog, we will focus on the default segmentation determination in documents such as Sales Order, Purchase Orders, etc.

There are multiple scenarios where you would want the system to default the segmentation value in documents instead of users manually them in the documents. To achieve this, you can set up default segmentation values for desired documents.

Supported Documents:

Default Requirement segment (Demand) determination is supported in Sales Orders, Stock Transport Orders, Stock Transport Requisitions, Safety Stock and Planned Independent Requirements.

Default Stock segment (Supply) determination is supported in Purchase Orders, Stock Transport Orders, Purchase Requisitions, Planned Orders, Production Orders, Process Orders and Outline Agreements.

Let us take three common scenarios where default segment value is required in sales orders and purchase orders.

Scenario 1: Default Requirement Segment Determination in Sales Orders.

Default requirement segment sales order based on sales area and customer group.

Step 1: Maintain default determination rules

Using SGT_DETAULT Transaction

  1. Go to transaction SGT_DEFAULT and click on "Change"
  2. Double click the corresponding the segmentation strategy and go to "Sales Order" Tab
  3. Click on "Add New Lines" and enter respective data as shown below

  1. You can specify segment values directly or based on document field values. In this case, we will use “value-based” option determination.

Note – The validity dates are checked against the first delivery date in the sales order item.

  1. Click Save

Using “Manage Default Segmentation Rules” Fiori App (F9070)

  1. Go to Fiori App “Manage Default Segmentation Rules”
  2. Enter Segmentation strategy. Go to “Sales Order” tab and click on “Create”

  1. Click on save

Step 2: Go to Sales order and enter the desired data

You will observe that requirement segment is determined in the sales order.

 Scenario 2: Default Stock Segment Determination in Purchase Orders.

Default requirement segment in Purchase Order based on sales area/document type/purchasing org/purchasing group.

Step 1: Maintain default determination rules

Using SGT_DETAULT Transaction

  1. Go to transaction SGT_DEFAULT and click on ‘Change’
  2. Double click the corresponding the segmentation strategy and go to “Purchase Order: Stock Segment” Tab
  3. Click on ‘Add New Lines’ and enter respective data as shown below

Note – The validity dates are checked against the delivery date in the purchase order item.

  1. Click Save

Using “Manage Default Segmentation Rules” Fiori App (F9070)

  1. Go to Fiori App “Manage Default Segmentation Rules”
  2. Enter Segmentation strategy. Go to “Purchase Order: Stock Segment” tab and click on “Create”

  1. Click on save

Step 2: Go to Purchase order and enter the desired data

You will observe that stock segment is determined in the purchase order.

Scenario 3: Default Requirement Segment Determination in Sales Orders using sales order field (e.g. Order Reason)

Step 1: Go to Fiori App “Manage Default Segmentation Catalogs” (F9069)

  1. Go to “Sales Order” Tab
  2. Check for the fields listed under the corresponding such as VBAK, VBAP and if it doesn’t exist, click on “Add”
  3. Select the desired field and click on “Select”

  1. Once desired fields(s) are added, click on “Synchronize”

Step 2: Maintain Default segment based on Order reason

  1. Go to Fiori App “Manage Default Segmentation Rules”
  2. Click on “Add”
  3. In the “Additional Fields” section, select the Order reason from the catalog

Step 3: Enter desired data in the sales order and observe that requirement segment is determined based on the default set up.

Next blog will focus on segmentation consideration in Advanced ATP processes.

SAP Help Documentation:

  1. Define Default Segment Values
  2. Manage Default Segmentation Catalogs App
  3. Manage Default Segmentation Rules App

Blog Series:

  1. Demand and Supply Segmentation in S/4 HANA - Segmentation Setup
  2. Default Segmentation Determination in documents (Blog 2)

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere – Replication Flow – Loading delta data from SAP S/4HANA – Q & A Session
entry.content:
Hello again Rajeshwar, 

The main reason is that Analytic Model is designed for multidimensional analysis and consumption, so the ability to export to Excel is not restricted.  While views and tables in Data Builder are intermediate modeling artifacts for design-time modeling, so I believe the restriction is by design.  Export of data that is not consumption-ready are not allow for performance reason.  As a customer, this would be a good enhancement request that you can take to SAP support for the reason you specified.

entry.title: Re: CF SSH - the tunnel to SAP-BTP: CF, HANA, Postgresql
entry.content:
@sap_sabarna 

Blog title shows HANA, Postgres but in your blog content talks about SAP Hana Cloud and nothing about Postgres.... entry.title: Managing SAP AI Core from the Terminal with saic-cli
entry.content:
Managing SAP AI Core from the Terminal with saic-cli

Working with SAP AI Core day-to-day surfaces a few recurring pain points with SAP AI Launchpad:

  • No way to create custom scenarios through the UI
  • No bulk operations for prompt templates — creating or cleaning up templates means doing it one by one
  • Repetitive UI navigation for routine tasks like listing configurations or checking deployment status
  • No scripting support — any automation requires building custom API calls from scratch

saic-cli is a small CLI tool that brings all of this to the terminal — no UI, no manual API calls, just commands.

Getting Started

1. Install

npm install -g sap-aicore-cli

2. Connect to Your AI Core Instance

The setup command wraps cf login and stores your credentials:

saic-cli setup --sso -a https://api.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

This is the only configuration you need. From here, every command picks up your credentials automatically.

What Makes It Useful

Beyond the commands themselves, a few things make saic-cli practical for day-to-day work:

Full API coverage — deployments, executions, configurations, prompt templates, secrets, repositories, resource groups, dataset files, and more. If it's in the SAP AI Core REST API, there's a command for it.

Zero config after setup — one saic-cli setup command is all it takes. Every subsequent command picks up credentials automatically, no extra configuration needed.

Scriptable by default — every command supports --json output, making it easy to pipe results into other tools or use in shell scripts.

Safe to automate — --dry-run lets you preview any write operation before it runs, useful in CI/CD pipelines.

Manage Deployments

# List running deployments
saic-cli list-deployments --status RUNNING

# Create a deployment from a configuration
saic-cli create-deployment --config-id <config-id>

# Stop and clean up
saic-cli update-deployment <id> --target-status STOPPED
saic-cli delete-deployment <id> --force

Configure Foundation Models

Creating a configuration for a foundation model like GPT-4o is a single command:

saic-cli create-configuration \
  --name "gpt-4o" \
  --executable-id "azure-openai" \
  --scenario-id "foundation-models" \
  --params '[{"key":"modelName","value":"gpt-4o"}]'

Manage Prompt Templates in Bulk

One area where the Launchpad falls short is prompt template management. saic-cli provides a small workflow to go from zero to a deployed template quickly.

Start by generating a ready-to-edit template scaffold:

saic-cli generate-template

This creates a folder with everything you need:

my-template/
├── prompt.yaml         # Main config: name, version, scenario, variables, LLM settings
├── system-prompt.md    # System role instructions (edit to define AI behavior)
└── user-prompt.md      # User role template with variable placeholders e.g. {{?InputText}}

The prompt.yaml looks like this:

name: MY_TEMPLATE
version: 1.0.0
scenario: MY_SCENARIO

spec:
  template:
    - role: system
      contentFile: ./system-prompt.md
    - role: user
      contentFile: ./user-prompt.md
  variable_definition:
    InputText: ""
    OutputFormat: ""
  additional_fields:
    executableId: azure-openai
    llmName: gpt-4o

Edit the files to fit your use case, then do a dry run before deploying:

saic-cli create-prompt --dir ./my-template --dry-run

Once ready, deploy:

saic-cli create-prompt --dir ./my-template

You can also list and clean up templates by scenario:

# List templates in a scenario
saic-cli list-templates --scenario-id my-scenario

# Delete all templates in a scenario
saic-cli delete --scenario my-scenario

Scripting & Automation

Every command supports --json output, which makes it easy to pipe results into other tools:

# Get all running deployments as JSON
saic-cli list-deployments --status RUNNING --json

# Preview a command before running it
saic-cli create-deployment --config-id <id> --dry-run

The --dry-run flag is particularly useful in CI/CD pipelines — you can validate inputs without making any changes.

Other Commands

Beyond deployments and prompts, the CLI covers:

  • Executions & schedules — create, update, delete, and schedule AI executions
  • Secrets — manage Docker registry, object store, and generic secrets
  • Repositories & applications — onboard git repos and manage ArgoCD applications
  • Resource groups & services — full lifecycle management
  • Dataset files — upload, download, and delete files from object storage

Run saic-cli --help to see the full list.

Project Structure

saic-cli is a TypeScript CLI where each command is implemented as a self-contained plugin. Commands are lazy-loaded, so only what's needed gets initialized. Under the hood it uses the @sap-ai-sdk packages to talk to SAP AI Core.

sap-aicore-cli/
├── src/
│   ├── cli.ts                  # Entry point, command routing
│   ├── index.ts                # Library exports
│   ├── commands/               # Command plugins (one file per command)
│   │   ├── setup.ts            # Interactive setup wizard
│   │   ├── deployments.ts      # Deployment CRUD
│   │   ├── executions.ts       # Execution management
│   │   ├── create-prompt.ts    # Prompt template creation
│   │   ├── generate-template.ts# Template scaffold generator
│   │   └── ...
│   ├── api/                    # API wrappers (one file per resource)
│   │   ├── deployments.ts
│   │   ├── prompt-registry.ts
│   │   └── ...
│   └── utils/                  # Shared utilities

Closing Thoughts

saic-cli is still early-stage and covers common day-to-day use cases — but if you're working with SAP AI Core and find yourself reaching for the same Launchpad screens repeatedly, it might save you some clicks.

Feedback and contributions are welcome.

Links

  • npm: sap-aicore-cli
  • GitHub: mduw/sap-aicore-cli
  • SAP AI Core REST APIs
